entry.title: 1st General Meeting of the Spanish EvoDevOmics Network in Barcelona 27-28 April 2026

link.title: 1st General Meeting of the Spanish EvoDevOmics Network in Barcelona 27-28 April 2026

entry.content:
We’re delighted to celebrate that Nacho and Rubén attended the General Meeting of the Spanish EvoDevOmics Network, where they presented a talk sharing their latest research. The meeting brought together researchers from across Spain focused on Evolutionary Developmental Biology, making it a great venue for discussion and exchange.

Congratulations to both for showcasing their work and representing the lab so well! 👏🧬
entry.summary: We’re delighted to celebrate that Nacho and Rubén attended the General Meeting of the Spanish EvoDevOmics Network, where they presented a talk sharing their latest research. The meeting brought together researchers from across Spain focused on Evolutionary Developmental Biology, making it a great venue for discussion and exchange.

entry.title: Welcome to the lab, Fabian!🎉🎉
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to welcome Fabian Lim to the Real Lab as a postdoctoral researcher.

At the Real Lab, Fabian will be exploring the fascinating biology of the Iberian mole, comparing the 3D genomic architecture and enhancers between moles and mice to uncover mechanisms of evolutionary adaptation behind specialized traits.
entry.summary: We’re thrilled to welcome Fabian Lim to the Real Lab as a postdoctoral researcher.

entry.title: Limb development and regeneration: quantitative, ecological, and diversification studies. March 2026, Tokyo (Japan) 🇯🇵🧬

link.title: Limb development and regeneration: quantitative, ecological, and diversification studies. March 2026, Tokyo (Japan) 🇯🇵🧬

entry.content:
Fany was an invited speaker at the congress Limb Development and Regeneration: Quantitative, Ecological, and Diversification Studies, held in Japan.

This EMBO Workshop focused on understanding how genetic programs, cellular dynamics, and environmental factors shape limb development, regeneration, and morphological diversification across species. By integrating quantitative approaches, evolutionary perspectives, and ecological insights, the meeting fostered cross‑disciplinary dialogue to advance our understanding of limb biology.

During the congress, Fany had the pleasure of delivering her first lecture as an EMBO Young Investigator Programme (EMBO‑YIP) member.
entry.summary: Fany was an invited speaker at the congress Limb Development and Regeneration: Quantitative, Ecological, and Diversification Studies, held in Japan.

entry.title: Symposium of Early Career Research Investigators of Seville in Biomedicine & Biology (SECRIS) 2026

link.title: Symposium of Early Career Research Investigators of Seville in Biomedicine & Biology (SECRIS) 2026

entry.content:
SECRIS (Seville Early Career Research Investigators Symposium) is an initiative created to provide a shared meeting space for early‑career researchers from the three main biomedical research centres in Seville: IBiS, CABIMER, and CABD. Coordinated by Prof. Gabriel Núñez (University of Michigan / Visiting Professor at IBiS) and organised by predoctoral researchers from the three centres, the symposium aims to showcase early‑career research, promote interaction, and foster new scientific collaborations across institutions in the city.

Building on the success of its first edition, the goal of SECRIS 2026 is to further strengthen inter‑institutional connections among early‑career scientists in Seville, encourage interdisciplinary dialogue, and consolidate a sustainable local network that supports collaboration, visibility, and career development for emerging researchers.

Keren and Miguel (Lupiáñez Lab), and Nacho and Rubén (Real Lab), actively participated as members and organisers within the SECRIS committee, contributing to the coordination and development of the symposium and reinforcing the collaborative spirit of the initiative.
entry.summary: SECRIS (Seville Early Career Research Investigators Symposium) is an initiative created to provide a shared meeting space for early‑career researchers from the three main biomedical research centres in Seville: IBiS, CABIMER, and CABD. Coordinated by Prof. Gabriel Núñez (University of Michigan / Visiting Professor at IBiS) and organised by predoctoral researchers from the three centres, the symposium aims to showcase early‑career research, promote interaction, and foster new scientific collaborations across institutions in the city.

entry.title: Kick-off meeting of the EvoDevOmics Spanish Network in Valencia! 🇪🇸🧬

link.title: Kick-off meeting of the EvoDevOmics Spanish Network in Valencia! 🇪🇸🧬

entry.content:
Fany traveled to Valencia to meet with the EvoDevOmics Spanish Network 🇪🇸🧬.

A great opportunity to strengthen collaborations, share progress, and explore new ideas in evolutionary genetics and developmental biology.
entry.summary: Fany traveled to Valencia to meet with the EvoDevOmics Spanish Network 🇪🇸🧬.

entry.title: Fany now is an EMBO Young Investigator!! 🎉🎉

link.title: Fany now is an EMBO Young Investigator!! 🎉🎉

entry.content:
We are thrilled to announce that Fany (Francisca Martínez Real), has been selected as one of the 28 new EMBO Young Investigators! 🎉

The EMBO Young Investigator Programme (YIP) is a prestigious initiative that supports outstanding young group leaders in the life sciences during the critical early stages of establishing their independent research careers. Selected investigators become part of an international network of excellence, gaining access to mentoring, leadership training, and opportunities for collaboration. The programme also provides financial support, annual funds for networking and travel, as well as access to EMBL core facilities. Being part of EMBO YIP is a mark of scientific distinction and a gateway to resources that foster innovation and leadership in research.

For more information check 📰:

We also had a proper celebration!! 🍾🍰
entry.summary: We are thrilled to announce that Fany (Francisca Martínez Real), has been selected as one of the 28 new EMBO Young Investigators! 🎉

entry.title: Nacho CABD Meeting
entry.content:
🎉 Nacho CABD Meeting 🧬

This Monday, our PhD student Nacho had the opportunity to present the latest progress on his thesis project at the CABD meeting. His talk is titled:

“Functional genomics of mole forelimb adaptation: a synthetic biology approach”

Here you have the summary of the talk:

Understanding how genomic mutations drive phenotypic evolution is a central question in modern biology. One striking example is the specialized morphology of mole forelimbs, adapted to fossorial environments. Our preliminary data suggest that these adaptations are associated with changes in the 3D-chromatin organization, regulation, and expression of the HOXD gene cluster, a key regulator of limb development. However, directly testing the functional relevance of these regulatory changes remains a major technological challenge. The aim of this project is to employ synthetic biology to reconstruct regulatory modifications observed in the mole HOXD cluster within transgenic mouse models, and to assess their phenotypic consequences in vivo. By introducing these specific regulatory variants into a controlled genetic background, we will be able to determine how such alterations impact gene expression and limb development. This approach provides a unique opportunity to experimentally validate the contribution of regulatory evolution to morphological diversification. Ultimately, this work seeks to bridge the gap between observations from comparative genomics analyses and their functional validation, offering insights into the mechanisms by which regulatory changes shape evolutionary novelties. Beyond its relevance to evolutionary biology, this methodology could establish a versatile framework for modeling evolutionary processes in vivo through synthetic genomic engineering.

entry.title: Nacho participated in the Principles of Evolution III organized by LifeHUB (CSIC) 🐾

link.title: Nacho participated in the Principles of Evolution III organized by LifeHUB (CSIC) 🐾

entry.content:
Nacho participated in the Principles of Evolution III: Biology through Time and Organizational Levels course organized by LifeHUB (CSIC).

The course took place at the Doñana Biological Reserve, in Doñana National Park (Huelva, Spain), from 3 to 14 November 2025. During the course, Nacho attended lectures and discussions by researchers in evolutionary biology covering topics such as evolutionary processes across different timescales, levels of biological organization, and comparative perspectives across species. The program combined theoretical sessions with practical activities in a unique natural environment. 🏞️

As part of the program, Nacho gave a short presentation introducing his PhD thesis project, which was a valuable opportunity to receive feedback and discuss his research with both faculty and other participants. The course also provided him a great chance to meet other early-career researchers, exchange ideas, and build professional connections in a collaborative and interdisciplinary setting.
entry.summary: Nacho participated in the Principles of Evolution III: Biology through Time and Organizational Levels course organized by LifeHUB (CSIC).

entry.title: Learning HCR with Keren Yam from Lupiañez Lab

link.title: Learning HCR with Keren Yam from Lupiañez Lab

entry.content:
We are excited to share that we are currently training with Keren to master In situ Hybridization Chain Reaction (HCR) 🔬.

What is HCR? HCR is a in situ hybridization method that allows to visualize the spatial distribution of gene expression directly within intact tissues. Unlike traditional methods, HCR uses a chain reaction of hybridization events to amplify the signal, providing high sensitivity and clear localization of target transcripts.

Here you can see one of our first images 🎉!

Stay tuned for updates and images from this exciting work!
entry.summary: We are excited to share that we are currently training with Keren to master In situ Hybridization Chain Reaction (HCR) 🔬.

entry.title: Fany as Invited speaker at 2025 Louis-Jeantet Symposium

link.title: Fany as Invited speaker at 2025 Louis-Jeantet Symposium

entry.content:
As part of the invited speakers, Fany presented her talk: Elucidating the molecular basis of mammalian flight

Bats are the only mammals capable of self-powered flight, an evolutionary innovation based on the transformation of forelimbs into wings. The bat wing is characterized by an extreme elongation of the second to fifth digits and a wing membrane called chiropatagium connecting them. This adaptation enabled bats to conquer the aerial environment, contributing to the massive diversification of this taxonomic group. Yet, the molecular underpinnings of this evolutionary innovation remain obscure. In this study, we have explored the molecular basis of bat wing formation through a suite of genomic tools and single-cell analyses. We found that despite the substantial morphological differences between the species, there is an overall conservation of cell populations and gene expression patterns, including interdigital apoptosis. To trace the cellular origin of the chiropatagium, we performed single-cell and label transfer analyses on manually micro-dissected cells and identified a distinct fibroblast population, independent of the apoptosis-associated interdigital cells, as the source of this tissue. These distal cells express a conserved gene program including the transcription factors MEIS2 and TBX3, which are commonly known to specify and pattern the early proximal limb. Transgenic ectopic expression of MEIS2 and TBX3 in mouse distal limb cells resulted in the activation of genes expressed during wing development and phenotypic changes related to wing morphology, such as the fusion of digits. Our results elucidate fundamental molecular mechanisms of bat wing development and illustrate how drastic morphological changes can be achieved through repurposing of existing developmental programs during evolution.

If you want to know more about the symposium you can check their website: (Louis-Jeantet Symposium info)
entry.summary: As part of the invited speakers, Fany presented her talk: Elucidating the molecular basis of mammalian flight
